title: 15_train_serve_skew.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: One Artifact, One Truth for the Turbine Failure Model

The predictive-maintenance team trains a Ridge regressor that estimates hours-to-failure for wind-turbine gearboxes. Training standardizes the telemetry (rotor RPM in the thousands, oil temperature in the tens) before fitting, then publishes an artifact that the prediction API loads in a separate container. The release gate is a train/serve parity check: push one known row through the training path and the serving path — the two predictions must agree to within float noise.

The gate is solid red. The training path says a healthy 312.4 hours; the serving path returns a physically impossible -5217.6 hours for the *same row*. Nothing about the model weights changed between the two calls — which means the two paths are not running the same computation. Find where the contract between them breaks.
initialCode:
import joblib
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

df = pd.read_csv("turbine_telemetry.csv")
X = df.drop(columns=["hours_to_failure"]).values
y = df["hours_to_failure"].values

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)
model = Ridge(alpha=1.0).fit(X_scaled, y)
joblib.dump(model, "artifacts/model.joblib")

# serving path: what the prediction API runs per request
served = joblib.load("artifacts/model.joblib")
request_row = X[[0]]
train_pred = model.predict(X_scaled[[0]])[0]
serve_pred = served.predict(request_row)[0]
print(f"train path: {train_pred:.1f}h | serve path: {serve_pred:.1f}h")
assert abs(train_pred - serve_pred) < 1e-6, f"train/serve skew: {train_pred:.1f}h vs {serve_pred:.1f}h"
traceback:
train path: 312.4h | serve path: -5217.6h
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 21
     19 serve_pred = served.predict(request_row)[0]
     20 print(f"train path: {train_pred:.1f}h | serve path: {serve_pred:.1f}h")
---> 21 assert abs(train_pred - serve_pred) < 1e-6, f"train/serve skew: {train_pred:.1f}h vs {serve_pred:.1f}h"

AssertionError: train/serve skew: 312.4h vs -5217.6h

options.label: Persist the scaler as a second artifact and standardize each request with scaler.fit_transform in the API
options.patchCode:
import joblib
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

df = pd.read_csv("turbine_telemetry.csv")
X = df.drop(columns=["hours_to_failure"]).values
y = df["hours_to_failure"].values

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)
model = Ridge(alpha=1.0).fit(X_scaled, y)
joblib.dump(model, "artifacts/model.joblib")
joblib.dump(scaler, "artifacts/scaler.joblib")

# serving path: what the prediction API runs per request
served = joblib.load("artifacts/model.joblib")
served_scaler = joblib.load("artifacts/scaler.joblib")
request_row = X[[0]]
train_pred = model.predict(X_scaled[[0]])[0]
serve_pred = served.predict(served_scaler.fit_transform(request_row))[0]
print(f"train path: {train_pred:.1f}h | serve path: {serve_pred:.1f}h")
assert abs(train_pred - serve_pred) < 1e-6, f"train/serve skew: {train_pred:.1f}h vs {serve_pred:.1f}h"
options.resultLog:
train path: 312.4h | serve path: 304.9h
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 23
     21 serve_pred = served.predict(served_scaler.fit_transform(request_row))[0]
     22 print(f"train path: {train_pred:.1f}h | serve path: {serve_pred:.1f}h")
---> 23 assert abs(train_pred - serve_pred) < 1e-6, f"train/serve skew: {train_pred:.1f}h vs {serve_pred:.1f}h"

AssertionError: train/serve skew: 312.4h vs 304.9h
options.rationale: Shipping the scaler is half the answer — but `fit_transform` at serve time re-estimates statistics from a batch of exactly one row. The mean of one sample is the sample itself, sklearn maps the resulting zero variances to a scale of 1, and every request standardizes to the all-zeros vector. The model therefore predicts its intercept — the training mean of y, here 304.9 — for every turbine, regardless of the telemetry. A constant predictor that answers 200 OK is the worst kind of broken. Frozen statistics must be applied with `transform`, never re-learned per request — and even then, two separately versioned artifacts leave room to drift apart.

options.label: Drop the scaler from training so the model and the API both consume raw features
options.patchCode:
import joblib
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge

df = pd.read_csv("turbine_telemetry.csv")
X = df.drop(columns=["hours_to_failure"]).values
y = df["hours_to_failure"].values

model = Ridge(alpha=1.0).fit(X, y)
joblib.dump(model, "artifacts/model.joblib")

# serving path: what the prediction API runs per request
served = joblib.load("artifacts/model.joblib")
request_row = X[[0]]
train_pred = model.predict(request_row)[0]
serve_pred = served.predict(request_row)[0]
print(f"train path: {train_pred:.1f}h | serve path: {serve_pred:.1f}h")
assert abs(train_pred - serve_pred) < 1e-6, f"train/serve skew: {train_pred:.1f}h vs {serve_pred:.1f}h"
options.rationale: The gate turns green, which is exactly the trap: parity only certifies that both paths run the same computation, not that the computation is any good. You achieved parity by deleting the preprocessing — and Ridge's L2 penalty now shrinks coefficients according to raw units rather than signal, so RPM-scale features dominate the fit while temperature-scale ones are crushed toward zero. Validation error rises, no assert notices, and the quality regression ships behind a passing check. Never satisfy an integrity gate by removing the thing it was protecting.

options.label: Fit and persist one Pipeline (scaler + model) so the artifact carries its own preprocessing
options.patchCode:
import joblib
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

df = pd.read_csv("turbine_telemetry.csv")
X = df.drop(columns=["hours_to_failure"]).values
y = df["hours_to_failure"].values

pipeline = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), Ridge(alpha=1.0))
pipeline.fit(X, y)
joblib.dump(pipeline, "artifacts/model.joblib")

# serving path: what the prediction API runs per request
served = joblib.load("artifacts/model.joblib")
request_row = X[[0]]
train_pred = pipeline.predict(request_row)[0]
serve_pred = served.predict(request_row)[0]
print(f"train path: {train_pred:.1f}h | serve path: {serve_pred:.1f}h")
assert abs(train_pred - serve_pred) < 1e-6, f"train/serve skew: {train_pred:.1f}h vs {serve_pred:.1f}h"
options.rationale: A `Pipeline` makes the preprocessing part of the model object itself: one `fit` learns the scaler statistics and the Ridge coefficients together, and one `joblib.dump` serializes them as a single unit. The API's `predict` now runs standardize-then-regress with the exact training-time means and variances by construction — they are literally the same serialized numbers — so there is no second artifact to forget, version-skew, or misuse. Train/serve parity stops being a discipline someone must remember and becomes a structural property of the artifact.

hints: Weights learned on standardized features are only meaningful in standardized coordinates. Trace what actually travels from the training process to the serving container — does everything a prediction needs survive the trip?

Lines 10–13: the scaler is fitted as a free-standing object, but the `joblib.dump` on line 13 serializes only `model`. The fitted means and variances never reach the serving side — sklearn has a way to make them part of the model object itself.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
The Ridge coefficients were learned against standardized telemetry, so each weight means "change in predicted hours per standard deviation of that sensor." But `joblib.dump(model, ...)` on line 13 serializes only the estimator — the fitted scaler, which holds the means and variances that define those units, dies with the training process. The serving container loads the bare model and feeds it raw telemetry: RPM values in the thousands hit coefficients calibrated for inputs near ±2, the dot product explodes, and the API returns -5217.6 hours for a row the training path scores at 312.4. The parity assert is doing its job — the two paths genuinely compute different functions.

### Why the fix is correct
`make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), Ridge(alpha=1.0))` fuses preprocessing and estimator into a single fitted object, so the one `joblib.dump` call captures everything a prediction needs. When the API calls `served.predict(raw_row)`, the pipeline replays the exact training-time standardization — same means, same variances, byte-identical because they are the same serialized numbers — before the regression sees the data. Parity stops depending on two codebases agreeing forever and becomes a guarantee built into the artifact: serialize the whole function, not just its last layer.
recruiterReview: This is the failure mode that separates notebook ML from production ML, and you nailed the root cause: the coefficients are only meaningful in the coordinate system the scaler defined, and that coordinate system never made it into the artifact. Persisting one Pipeline is the right fix — not "dump the scaler too," which leaves two artifacts free to version-skew, and certainly not weakening the gate. I also like that the parity assert caught this before a customer did; that check stays in CI for every artifact we publish. Forward-looking: pin the sklearn version in the serving image and add a golden-prediction test against a frozen artifact, so a library upgrade can't reintroduce skew silently. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Train/Serve Skew: When Two Paths Compute Two Models

A trained model is not just its weights. It is the weights *plus every transformation that shaped the matrix those weights were fitted on* — scaling, encoding, imputation, feature order, even the dtype coercions a DataFrame applied silently. Production systems split training and serving across processes, containers, and often teams, and **train/serve skew** is what happens when the two sides stop running the same computation. It is among the most expensive bug classes in ML because nothing crashes: the API stays up, returns well-formed numbers, and is wrong.

### The artifact is the contract
Think of the serialized artifact as the only message training is allowed to send to serving. Anything a prediction needs that is *not inside the artifact* must be re-implemented on the serving side — perfectly, forever, through every refactor on either side. A fitted `StandardScaler` holds the training means and variances; a fitted encoder holds the category vocabulary; an imputer holds its fill values. If the artifact carries only the estimator, those learned statistics evaporate when the training process exits, and the serving path is left guessing.

### How skew sneaks in
The common shapes: preprocessing fitted as free-standing objects and never persisted (this challenge); preprocessing persisted as a *second* artifact that version-drifts from the model it was fitted for; preprocessing re-implemented by hand in another language for the API ("it is just a subtract and a divide") that diverges on edge cases; feature order or column subsets that differ between the training frame and the request payload. Every one of them produces the same symptom — predictions that are plausible-looking garbage — and none of them throws an exception.

### Design it away: one fitted object
The sklearn `Pipeline` exists to make the contract structural. `make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), Ridge())` is a single estimator: `fit` learns the scaler statistics and the model coefficients in one call, `predict` replays the standardization before the regression, and one `joblib.dump` serializes the whole computation. The serving side cannot skip the preprocessing because it does not even know it is there. The idea generalizes beyond sklearn: TensorFlow ships preprocessing layers inside SavedModels, and ONNX graphs can embed the transform. The principle is portable — *serialize the function, not just its last layer*.

### Verify it: parity tests
Because skew is silent, you test for it explicitly. A parity (golden-prediction) test pushes a frozen set of known rows through the training-time path and the serving path and asserts the outputs match to float tolerance. Run it in CI on every artifact you publish and on every serving-image rebuild. It is cheap, it catches scaler drift, library-version drift, and feature-order drift alike, and when it fires — as in this challenge — it points at the contract, not at the model. Pin library versions in the serving image too: a pickle loaded under a different sklearn can compute differently long before it warns.

### Common variations of this bug
- A scaler, encoder, or imputer fitted in the training script but never serialized with the model.
- Preprocessing saved as a second artifact that falls out of sync with the model version it was fitted for.
- Serving code re-implementing the transform by hand (different language, "simplified" math) and diverging on edge cases.
- Feature columns ordered differently between the training frame and the serving request payload.
- Vocabulary or category mappings rebuilt at serve time, so unseen-category handling differs from training.
- Library-version drift between training and serving images changing a transform's numerical behavior.
